title: Reward theft via lpShares/tokenAmount reward accounting mismatch when pool is skewed by orderbook operations
claim: The claimReward function computes accumulated rewards using mm.lpShares (line 236), but provideLiquidity and withdrawLiquidity set rewardDebt based on mm.tokenAmount + mm.ethAmount (lines 150-153, 206-208). These two units diverge whenever the orderbook changes pool composition via transferETHToOrderbook/transferTokenToOrderbook/receiveETHFromOrderbook/receiveTokenFromOrderbook, which alter pool.totalLiquidity without changing pool.totalLPShares. A subsequent liquidity provider receives lpShares = contribution × totalLPShares / totalLiquidity, which exceeds their contribution when totalLiquidity < totalLPShares. The attacker can then call claimReward and extract accumulated rewards proportional to (lpShares − tokenAmount − ethAmount) × accRewardPerShare, stealing rewards that were earned by existing LPs. The stolen ETH comes from the same ETH pool that backs LP withdrawals, potentially leaving the contract insolvent for legitimate LPs.
impact: An attacker can steal accumulated rewards from existing LPs by depositing liquidity after the orderbook creates a favorable pool skew. The attack requires no privileged access. In realistic scenarios (token price > 1 ETH, sell orders), a 25% skew yields ~3.3 ETH stolen per 200 ETH deposited when 100 ETH of rewards are in the pool. The attack scales linearly with deposit size and reward pool size. This is a systemic accounting error that affects every pool and every LP, not a single-point exploit.
paths: 1. LP deposits liquidity when pool is balanced (totalLiquidity = totalLPShares).
2. Rewards are distributed via receiveFeeDistribution, increasing accRewardPerShare.

3. Orderbook processes sell orders at price > 1 ETH (e.g., transferETHToOrderbook(200 ETH) + receiveTokenFromOrderbook(100 tokens)), causing totalLiquidity < totalLPShares (skew factor 0.75 in example).
4. Attacker calls provideLiquidity with ETH + tokens matching the Uniswap V2 spot ratio. The attacker receives lpShares = contribution × totalLPShares / totalLiquidity > contribution.

5. Attacker calls claimReward: accumulated = lpShares × accRewardPerShare / SCALE, rewardDebt = contribution × accRewardPerShare / SCALE. The difference (lpShares − contribution) × accRewardPerShare / SCALE is stolen from existing LPs.

Computed optimal example: pool with 2000 LP shares, 100 ETH rewards distributed, skew factor 0.75 (totalLiquidity=1500 vs totalLPShares=2000). Attacker deposits 200 ETH-equivalent (100 ETH + 100 tokens), gets 266.67 lpShares, claims 3.33 ETH of stolen rewards immediately.

title: Withdrawal uses pool totals for actual transfer but user balance tracking uses shares percentage — causing persistent accounting drift
claim: In withdrawLiquidity, the actual token/ETH amounts transferred to the user are computed from pool totals proportional to lpShares burned (lines 186-189): actualTokenWithdraw = pool.totalToken × lpSharesToBurn / pool.totalLPShares. However, the user's internal balance tracking (mm.tokenAmount, mm.ethAmount) is reduced by shares/10000 of the user's recorded amounts (lines 192-193): userTokenReduction = mm.tokenAmount × shares / 10000. These two calculations give the same result only when the user's lpShares proportion exactly matches their tokenAmount+ethAmount proportion relative to pool totals — which fails whenever the orderbook has changed pool composition without updating individual LP state. After orderbook operations shift the ETH/token ratio in the pool, partial withdrawals cause the user's tracked tokenAmount and ethAmount to drift from their actual economic stake. This corrupted state then feeds into reward calculations (userLiquidity = tokenAmount + ethAmount on lines 129 and 177), compounding the F-001 reward theft.
impact: Every partial withdrawal during or after orderbook activity causes the user's internal accounting (mm.tokenAmount, mm.ethAmount) to permanently diverge from their real pool share represented by lpShares. This poisons all future reward computations for that user, creating unpredictable over/under-payment of rewards. Combined with F-001, this enables multi-step attacks where an attacker strategically times deposits and partial withdrawals to maximize reward extraction at other LPs' expense.
paths: 1. Orderbook processes a trade that changes pool ETH/token composition (e.g., transferETHToOrderbook reduces pool.totalEth).
2. LP calls withdrawLiquidity with a partial shares value (e.g., 5000 = 50%).
3. The LP's actual withdrawal (pool.totalToken × lpSharesToBurn / pool.totalLPShares) does not match the user's balance reduction (mm.tokenAmount × 5000 / 10000).
4. The LP's mm.tokenAmount and mm.ethAmount are now out of sync with their true pool share.
5. On next provideLiquidity or withdrawLiquidity call, pending rewards are calculated using the corrupted userLiquidity value, leading to incorrect reward accrual.

title: Missing isNotBlocked modifier on provideLiquidity allows liquidity provision for blocked tokens
claim: The provideLiquidity function (line 105) is the only external state-changing function that lacks the isNotBlocked(token) modifier (defined at lines 60-63). All orderbook-facing functions (transferETHToOrderbook, transferTokenToOrderbook, receiveETHFromOrderbook, receiveTokenFromOrderbook) properly enforce isNotBlocked. This allows users to deposit liquidity into pools for tokens that the protocol owner has explicitly blocked via the GradientRegistry. While the blocked tokens cannot receive reward distributions (receiveFeeDistribution also lacks isNotBlocked but has onlyRewardDistributor), user funds deposited into blocked token pools may become illiquid or stuck if the token is blocked for security reasons (e.g., a hacked or depegged token).
impact: Users can deposit tokens into pools that the protocol governance has deemed unsafe, exposing themselves to risk and potentially interfering with the protocol's security response. In an emergency scenario where a token is blocked due to an exploit, attackers could still provide liquidity to manipulate pool state. The funds may become unrecoverable if the token is permanently blocked and no withdrawal path exists that bypasses the orderbook's blocked-token checks.
paths: 1. Protocol owner blocks a token via GradientRegistry.setTokenBlockStatus(token, true).
2. User (or attacker) calls provideLiquidity for the blocked token — the call succeeds because no isNotBlocked check exists.

3. The user's funds are now locked in a pool for a blocked token. The orderbook will refuse to process orders for this token (validToken modifier checks blockedTokens), so no organic trading or reward distribution will occur.
4. The user can only exit via withdrawLiquidity (which also lacks isNotBlocked), but receives pool assets at the current internal ratio which may be unfavorable.

title: Unprotected receive() function locks user-sent ETH with no recovery path except owner emergency withdrawal
claim: The contract exposes a payable receive() function (line 380) that accepts ETH from any sender without updating any pool accounting (totalEth, totalLiquidity, rewardBalance). ETH sent via receive() becomes part of address(this).balance but is invisible to the pool's accounting system — it cannot be withdrawn by LPs, claimed as rewards, or transferred by the orderbook as pool ETH. The only way to recover these funds is via the owner-only emergencyWithdraw() or emergencyWithdrawETH() functions.
impact: Any user or contract that accidentally sends ETH to the pool contract via a simple .transfer() or .send() call irrevocably loses those funds unless the owner chooses to return them. There is no on-chain mechanism for the sender to recover their ETH. This violates the principle that users should not be able to permanently lose funds through simple mistakes that the contract could prevent.
paths: 1. User or external contract sends ETH via .transfer() or .send() to the pool contract address.
2. The receive() function accepts the ETH with no revert and no state update.
3. The ETH is now part of address(this).balance but not tracked in pool.totalEth or any reward accounting.
4. LPs cannot withdraw this ETH (withdrawals are capped at pool.totalEth × lpShares / totalLPShares).
5. Reward claimants cannot receive this ETH (rewards are tracked via accRewardPerShare and rewardBalance).
6. Only the contract owner can recover it via emergencyWithdrawETH() or emergencyWithdraw().
